task: Write a Python function range_summary(nums) taking a list of integers, possibly unsorted with duplicates, and returning a string of the distinct values in ascending order, collapsing consecutive runs into 'start-end' and joining pieces with commas, so [1, 2, 3, 5] becomes '1-3,5'. A single number stays a single number, never a range. An empty list returns ''. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code correctly identifies and handles the edge case where an input list contains a single value.
trace:
**
I started by sorting the list and removing duplicates using `sorted(set(nums))`. Then, I iterated through the sorted list to identify consecutive sequences. For each sequence, if it is a single number, I added it as is; otherwise, I added the range in 'start-end' format. Finally, I joined all parts with commas to form the result string.

The edge case of an empty list was handled by returning an empty string at the beginning of the function.
